one_line_takeaway: A broad conceptual survey/position paper that defines the 'Agentic Web' via a three-dimension (Intelligence/Interaction/Economy) framework and contributes an original layered threat taxonomy (cognitive C1–C5, protocol P1–P6, economic E1–E5) with a cross-layer cascade model — conceptual, not empirical.
key_finding: The central thesis is that the Agentic Web is a qualitatively new Web phase requiring foundational rethinking of its algorithms, protocols, economics, and security — not incremental extension of existing systems. On security, the paper's own key claim is that agentic threats differ from traditional AI/network threats in scale and propagation: they cascade vertically (cognitive→protocol→economic), horizontally (agent-to-agent), and temporally (via persistent memory), so defenses must shift to zero-trust architectures, adaptive (not static) policies, and cascade prevention rather than incident isolation. In surveying evaluation, the authors highlight (as a cited finding, not their own experiment) that agentic-web safety is largely underexplored and that on Agent-SafetyBench no current agent scored above 60%.
core_contribution: This is a large (76-page) survey / position paper that proposes a structured conceptual framework for understanding and building the 'Agentic Web' — an internet phase in which autonomous LLM-powered agents act as intermediaries that plan, coordinate, and execute goal-directed tasks on behalf of users. The authors' OWN framework has several parts: (1) a three-era historical model of the Web (PC 'Search Paradigm' → Mobile 'Recommendation Paradigm' → Agentic 'Action Paradigm'), summarized in their cross-era comparison Table 1; (2) a three-dimension conceptual model of the Agentic Web — Intelligence, Interaction, and Economy; (3) three 'algorithmic transitions' (user-centric retrieval → agentic information retrieval; recommendation → agent planning; single-agent → multi-agent coordination); and (4) a system-level roadmap covering agent communication protocols (MCP, A2A). The security contribution that makes this on-topic is an original layered threat taxonomy for the Agentic Web (Section 7): the authors map novel threats onto their three architectural layers — Intelligence/cognitive (Table 5, threats C1–C5), Interaction/protocol (Table 6, P1–P6), and Value/economic (Table 7, E1–E5) — plus a 'risk evolution' contrast table (Table 4) and a 'cross-layer threat cascade' model (vertical/horizontal/temporal propagation). NOTE: the specific defenses, red-teaming methods, and benchmarks discussed later in Section 7 are surveyed prior work, not the authors' own systems.
threat_model: The paper frames a genuine, novel threat model for autonomous web agents. Adversary capability: attackers control web content, fake services/tools, service registries, and can act as malicious or compromised peer agents. Assets at risk: agent objectives/knowledge, cross-service authentication chains, user payment credentials, and real financial transactions. The authors argue the attack surface expands from 'API endpoints' to the 'entire web ecosystem' and that failure impact shifts from 'incorrect output' to 'real-world consequences' because agents gain transaction-execution authority, multi-session persistent memory, and cross-platform orchestration (Table 4). Concrete threat classes: persuasion-based goal drift (C1), knowledge-base poisoning via adversarial/SEO web content (C2), MCP context injection (P1), service-registry poisoning that harvests credentials (P2), A2A trust exploitation (P3), authentication-chain hijacking (P4), transaction-authority abuse (E1), and coordinated market manipulation (E5). The distinctive claim is that these threats CASCADE across layers (e.g., goal drift C1 → protocol exploit P1 → unauthorized purchase E1) and persist temporally through contaminated memory/training data, turning localized attacks into system-wide failures.
limitations: By its own nature the paper is speculative and forward-looking: many claims about the 'Agent Attention Economy,' agent-native markets, and cascade dynamics are hypotheses without empirical validation. The authors explicitly note that quantitative models for cascade probability/impact 'remain underdeveloped,' that certifiable/grounded defenses are largely absent, and that agentic-web safety evaluation is 'largely underexplored.' The threat taxonomy is illustrative (each threat has a single hypothetical example) rather than measured against real incidents at scale, and the paper does not benchmark or empirically compare any of the surveyed defenses itself. A reader must take care to separate the authors' original taxonomy from the many cited third-party systems and numbers reported in Section 7.

datasets_used: None used by the authors — this is a non-empirical survey/position paper with no experiments of its own.

Benchmarks discussed as SURVEYED prior work (not the paper's own): SafeArena, ST-WebAgentBench (built on WebArena), Agent-SafetyBench, Safe-OS (from AGrail), AgentDojo, VWAadv, Mind2Web-SC, EICU-AC, AdvWeb, EIA.
quantitative_results: The paper reports NO quantitative results of its own. All numbers below are CITED from surveyed third-party benchmarks/systems, not produced by the authors:
Agent-SafetyBench (cited): 349 interaction environments, 2,000 test cases, 8 safety risk categories; no evaluated agent scored above 60% on safety.
SafeArena (cited): 250 safe + 250 harmful tasks across harm categories (misinformation, cybercrime, social bias).
GuardAgent (cited): reported 98% accuracy on safety-critical tasks.
ST-WebAgentBench (cited): 6 policy dimensions (user consent, preference satisfaction, scope boundaries, strict execution, robustness to distribution shift, error recovery).
Microsoft Build 2025 (cited context): 50+ AI agent tools integrated across GitHub, Azure, Microsoft 365, Windows.
baselines_compared: None — the paper performs no empirical comparison and reports no head-to-head results of its own.
For context, its threat taxonomy is positioned qualitatively against two existing frameworks: OWASP Agentic AI Threat Model and CSA MAESTRO.
